Für den Einsatz in der öffentlichen Verwaltung sollen Leitfäden entwickelt und ein öffentlich einsehbares Register aller eingesetzten KI-Anwendungen eingerichtet werden. Die Brandenburgerinnen und Brandenburger sollen zudem über Beteiligungsformate aktiv in die Debatte über die Gestaltung und den Einsatz von KI eingebunden werden.

claims.stance_label: Human oversight with controlled AI deployment
claims.citation: State Secretary Bürger emphasized that 'Maki' would only be used as a support - a digital assistant, not a replacement for the judiciary. What the AI is supposed to do: automated analysis of files, suggestions for wording, text modules for court decisions. What it does not do: make judgments or decisions. The human being - the judge - remains ultimately responsible.

Im Fokus der Strategie stehen Maßnahmen wie der Ausbau von KI-Kompetenzen in Bildung und Verwaltung, die Mitarbeit an einer bundesweiten KI-Plattform für die Justiz sowie KI-basierte Anwendungen im Katastrophenschutz und in der Landwirtschaft. Ein öffentlich einsehbares Register aller KI-Anwendungen soll Transparenz schaffen, während Beteiligungsformate die Bürger einbeziehen.

claims.stance_label: AI risks taken seriously including catastrophic scenarios
claims.citation: In the Morgenbladet feature, the former group is represented mainly by Aksel Braanen Sterri, who is research director at the Oslo-based think tank Langsikt. He makes many interesting statements, but little or nothing that I am inclined to push back against.

The most powerful general systems are classified as posing a systemic risk, with similar requirements as high-risk applications. It is a long time before the legislation comes into force in Norway, but in Europe, areas with unacceptable risk will be banned already during the year.

claims.stance_label: Beneficial AI with catastrophic risk prevention
claims.citation: For example, we supported Langsikt - Centre for Long-Term Policy in producing evidence-based recommendations on beneficial AI for Norwegian policymakers.

A victory for both security and innovation for artificial intelligence in Europe. With higher requirements for those who make the most powerful models, there will be a greater regulatory burden on the largest players who have the best ability and resources to mitigate risk. At the same time, it will be easier for both small and large players to adopt the technology in a safe way.

claims.stance_label: Serious security and governance risks from agentic AI
claims.citation: Agentic AI governance is still in its early stages but imagine autonomous agents in the military that control weapons taking catastrophic actions before they can be stopped. Or picture a scenario where attackers could manipulate AI models into publishing people's private financial and health data.

claims.stance_label: Serious risks requiring balanced cautious approach
claims.citation: With big AI systems like ChatGPT, there are even bigger worries, like who gets to use all the data these AIs learn from, who holds the power in the AI world, and how much energy these things eat up. Plus, there's the whole issue of keeping things safe, especially when you think about national security and not letting sensitive info slip through the cracks.

The U.S. commitment to ethical, responsible use of AI is what makes it a leader in the AI frontier. Existing published AI principles include making sure that the AI is equitable and traceable. The alignment that the DOD and IC have on ethical AI guardrails enables them to innovate quickly and efficiently to ensure ethical AI.

AI governance needs to play a different role in mission delivery. It must control risk while simultaneously removing friction. By automating core governance processes, we're able to strengthen safeguards while reducing the barriers that have historically slowed AI adoption in complex, regulated environments.
Adopting a risk-based approach to AI governance and focusing on ethical considerations without stifling innovation is essential.

Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

No explicit public statements on AI regulation have been identified from Lemni Technologies. The company's focus on providing 'guardrails' within its platform suggests some awareness of responsible deployment, but no formal regulatory position has been articulated.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a machine-driven capability to achieve human-level or higher performance across most cognitive tasks.
claims.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (Also: General AI, Strong AI, Broad AI): Artificial general intelligence (AGI) describes a machine-driven capability to achieve human-level or higher performance across most cognitive tasks.
Submitted as "10-25 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

The rise of powerful AI will be either the best or the worst thing ever to happen to humanity. We do not yet know which. The research done by this centre will be crucial to the future of our civilisation and of our species.

AI poses or intersects with a range of safety and security challenges, some of which are immediate and some of which may only manifest in future as more powerful systems are developed and deployed in a wider range of societal settings... It also explores catastrophic risks associated with expected advances in frontier AI towards the industry goal of AGI, including the risks posed by increasing generality, agency and long time-horizon-planning.

The Leverhulme Trust funded the LCFI explicitly in response to concerns about risks posed by advanced AI, supporting research into 'responsible development of technology,' autonomous weapons, and existential risk. The Trust's call emphasized 'bold, disruptive thinking' to address challenges before they materialize, reflecting a precautionary orientation toward ensuring AI governance frameworks are developed proactively.

claims.definition_used: AGI as AI that has moved beyond narrow domain supremacy (chess, Go, protein folding) to match or exceed human performance broadly, emerging from the trajectory of increasingly capable LLMs.
claims.citation: While AI has already achieved supremacy in areas like chess, Go, and protein folding, its potential trajectory suggests even more profound and accelerated developments are on the horizon that could soon lead to artificial general intelligence (AGI).

claims.definition_used: AGI defined in the context of robotics as AI capable of performing 'any task, any robot, one brain' — general-purpose physical-world intelligence.
claims.citation: What does it look like to build toward AGI for the real world? Skild released their 2024 research results for the development of Skild Brain, taking us behind the scenes of their journey toward 'any task, any robot, one brain'.

While AI has already achieved supremacy in areas like chess, Go, and protein folding, its potential trajectory suggests even more profound and accelerated developments are on the horizon that could soon lead to artificial general intelligence (AGI).

At Lightspeed, we have long believed that AI will fundamentally redefine intelligence itself. Few frontiers are as compelling as scaling the autonomous capabilities of LLMs with reinforcement learning, and we believe Reflection AI is leading the charge with its breakthrough approach that brings AI closer to reasoning and problem-solving and, ultimately, superintelligence.

claims.stance_label: Serious but manageable with proactive investment
claims.citation: AI safety R&D (as with Rubrik in data security) is a NOW imperative, before there's a critical outage. The exponential rate of improvement in AI intelligence and the digital rails underlying vast amounts of our economic and societal activity, makes it impossible to predict the severity of AI-enabled cyber events that lie ahead.

claims.stance_label: Serious systemic risk from AI-enabled cyberattacks
claims.citation: There's a big, inevitable headline on the horizon: AI-enabled hackers will catch a major business unaware, exploit a vulnerability they wouldn't have been able to without AI, and make off with money or data, creating a viral news moment.

claims.stance_label: Anticipates a 'WannaCry moment' for AI safety
claims.citation: At some point we'll see a 'WannaCry moment' in AI, just as the 2017 ransomware attack forced enterprises to take cybersecurity seriously. When it comes, the world will demand the safety and alignment capabilities Anthropic has been building since day one.

A uniform national framework that avoids a fragmented, state-by-state patchwork that will likely lead to regulatory capture by incumbent technology companies is essential to sustaining the United States' advantages.

claims.stance_label: Baseline safety standards with governance frameworks
claims.citation: Whether it's through voluntary, state, or federal standards, we believe open, rigorous discussion about AI safety and arriving at a uniform set of baseline AI safety principles must happen in the broader ecosystem.

The company's focus appears growth-oriented and commercially driven, emphasizing accelerating Africa's digital transformation and AI adoption, which may suggest a light-touch or accelerationist preference, but this is not explicitly stated.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a future stage of AI development distinct from current systems, expected to emerge before technical alignment solutions can be achieved, after which Artificial Superintelligence (ASI) may follow.
claims.citation: Since I do not expect that we will achieve AI safety through technical solutions before artificial general intelligence emerges (AGI), I think technical AI governance is one of the best ways to contribute to a safer development of AGI (and probably one day Artificial Superintelligence, 'ASI').
She reduces her alignment timelines for the emergence of dangerous AI from 10 to 5 years based on technical considerations and alignment concerns.

Since I do not expect that we will achieve AI safety through technical solutions before artificial general intelligence emerges (AGI), I think technical AI governance is one of the best ways to contribute to a safer development of AGI.
The default trajectory of AI development has an unacceptably high likelihood of leading to human extinction. Critical research and policy is needed to correct.
She reduces her alignment timelines for the emergence of dangerous AI from 10 to 5 years based on technical considerations and alignment concerns.
If these systems were misaligned and not adequately controlled, this could lead to catastrophic and possibly existential outcomes.

Since I do not expect that we will achieve AI safety through technical solutions before artificial general intelligence emerges (AGI), I think technical AI governance is one of the best ways to contribute to a safer development of AGI (and probably one day Artificial Superintelligence, 'ASI').

Thiergart explains that MIRI believe that technical alignment is unlikely to be achieved pre-AGI, so they are instead working towards getting good regulation to buy time towards building the right technical solutions.

The default trajectory of AI development has an unacceptably high likelihood of leading to human extinction. Critical research and policy is needed to correct... We believe the main focus of this research should be ensuring humanity's collective ability to restrict dangerous AI development and deployment, up to and including a halt on frontier AI activities.

Their mission to 'nurture the AI startup ecosystem' and focus on 'disruptive AI solutions' suggests preference for enabling rather than restricting frameworks.

The foundation's funding of the USC Institute on Ethics & Trust in Computing suggests implicit support for ethical governance frameworks around AI, but no explicit regulatory stance has been publicly stated. Thomas Lord's founding philosophy emphasized private institutions over government as problem-solvers, which could suggest a preference for industry/academic self-governance over government regulation.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

With the field innovating at unprecedented rates, there is increasing urgency to develop novel evaluation methodologies allow full considerations of risks/threats of AI ologies in different domains... the call to action for this community is to pursue AI safety as a 'Big Science' project on a scale comparable to the Manhattan Project. High risks and high payoffs are on the table, but safe AI is a fast-moving target, and large-scale investments are needed to guide development of this technology in a responsible way.

"The potential upside to growing AI capabilities is endless," said Erick LeBrun, research scientist at Los Alamos. "However, measuring and understanding any potential dangers or misuse of advanced AI related to biological threats remain largely unexplored."

At the same time, AI poses risks that the nation will rely on the laboratory to address. The Office is responsible for: Partnering across the laboratory, with other laboratories, and with government stakeholders to develop a coherent national program aimed at mitigating risks from AI and at advancing science, technology, and security.

These new evaluations will support the recent White House Executive Order on the Safe, Secure and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence tasks where the Department of Energy national laboratories have been entrusted to help evaluate the capabilities of AI frontier models.

Coming to grips with frontier AI will depend on close partnerships with the private sector. Los Alamos has collaborated with industry for many years to develop technologies that meet social needs while bolstering national security. The Lab is expanding these collaborations to include analyzing AI's opportunities, unintended consequences, and threats.

Loti AI's CEO Luke Arrigoni has stated a vision of 'personal autonomy and technological progress existing in harmony,' and the company frames itself as building safety infrastructure for the generative AI economy. Their business model implicitly supports targeted regulation around likeness rights and deepfake misuse, positioning themselves as solving 'likeness and licensing challenges online,' suggesting support for structured digital identity protections without opposing AI development broadly.

No direct statements on AI regulation found, but Ding's sustained major funding of GPI, 80,000 Hours, and EA infrastructure — organizations centered on existential risk mitigation and careful prioritization of long-term harms — strongly suggests a precautionary orientation toward transformative technologies including AI. His own stated motivation is ensuring resources go toward preventing large-scale harm.

claims.definition_used: A true world model (and path to AGI) must combine understanding of the physical world with understanding of language, grasping architecture, physical motion, cause and effect — not merely making video models interactive.
claims.citation: He thinks that looks a lot like AGI. He also thinks most people in AI are still thinking too small to get there.

claims.definition_used: AGI (termed 'multimodal general intelligence') is AI that can generate, understand, and operate in the physical world, learning from video, image, audio, and language data — going beyond LLMs to simulate and reason about reality.
claims.citation: This Series C is a milestone in the company's mission to build multimodal general intelligence: AI that can generate, understand and operate in the physical world.

claims.definition_used: AGI requires a 'world model' — a system that combines understanding of the physical world with language understanding, grasping physics, causality, and long-range reasoning, enabling a model to reproduce, reason about, and operate within reality.
claims.citation: More broadly, Jain said world models are the next step towards artificial general intelligence, also known as superintelligence, which is the level when it reaches or exceeds human-level intelligence and the ability to self-teach.

claims.stance_label: No specific timeline given; world models a necessary step
claims.citation: Jain declined to say when he thought we would reach AGI, a topic that's still hotly debated with little consensus, but believes world models will play a role in getting there. 'A world model is a necessary condition, but not a sufficient condition for AGI,' he said.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

Luma AI has not made explicit public statements on AI regulation, but their messaging emphasizes rapid scaling of foundation models, fast deployment (Genie went live same day as Series B announcement), and the transformative potential of multimodal AI. Their focus is on building and deploying quickly, inferred from actions and investor communications.

As Microsoft's venture arm, M12's investment thesis is inferred from Microsoft's broader stance, which generally favors industry-led governance and targeted regulation over broad mandates. No explicit regulatory positions are stated in the sources; stance is inferred from corporate alignment with Microsoft's AI policy approach and investment focus on enterprise technology enablement.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a system that can pass a two-hour adversarial Turing test, assemble a model car, achieve 75%+ on MMLU, 90% average benchmark performance, and 90% top-1 accuracy on the APPS coding benchmark — a system capable of automating most jobs done from home.
claims.citation: One definition of AGI from a forecasting platform named Metaculus is that strong AGI has to pass a two-hour adversarial Turing test... Additionally, this system would need to have combined capabilities to assemble a model car, achieve 75% on the MMLU benchmark, and 90% on average. It must also achieve 90% top-1 accuracy on the APPS benchmark, which measures its coding ability.

AGI Timeline Consensus: Metaculus predicts strong AGI by mid-2033 based on adversarial Turing test criteria, while AI Futures forecasts 2030-2032 for automated coding and expert-level systems. Twenty percent chance exists by 2028, requiring front-loaded safety preparation despite median estimates suggesting more time remains for technical research and policy implementation.

If we expect AGI by 2031 and transformative AI by 2039, we're not close with the current growth rate of the field at 28% per year. MATS is part of this pipeline, transitioning informed talent who understand the scope of the problem into empowered talent who can work on the problem.
Our outreach and selection processes are designed to attract applicants who are motivated by reducing global catastrophic risk from AI.

What AI catastrophe could look like: What could an AI-caused existential catastrophe actually look like? ... Curriculum aimed to increase scholars' knowledge of AI safety ecosystem and potential catastrophe scenarios.

If we look at the risks of extinction—permanent destruction of 95% or more of the world's population—only artificial intelligence seems to be a really serious threat here. Bioengineering poses a 1% risk, while AI poses a 9% risk according to these prediction platforms.

MATS frames AI alignment as 'the world's most urgent and talent-constrained problem' and explicitly aims to reduce risks from unaligned AI, suggesting a precautionary stance prioritizing safety development before broader deployment. The program's expansion into governance and policy research tracks further reflects a proactive approach to AI risk management rather than reliance on market self-governance.

claims.entity_name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)
claims.definition_used: Intelligence is understood as how brains produce it and how it can be replicated in artificial systems to address real-world problems that exceed the capabilities of current AI technologies; the organization focuses on replicating human-level intelligence across domains rather than using the term 'AGI' explicitly.
claims.citation: SQI seeks to comprehend how brains produce intelligence and how it can be replicated in artificial systems to address real-world problems that exceed the capabilities of current artificial intelligence technologies.

claims.entity_name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)
claims.definition_used: Intelligence that can be replicated in artificial systems to address real-world problems beyond current artificial machine capabilities, framed as understanding how brains produce intelligence.
claims.citation: It is a quest for understanding intelligence –how brains produce it and how it can be replicated in artificial systems to address real world problems that are beyond current artificial machine capabilities. The MIT Quest approaches this as a single grand challenge requiring the organized, collaborative efforts of science, engineering, the humanities and beyond.

claims.entity_name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)
claims.definition_used: AI systems whose language, reasoning, and world knowledge capabilities align with those in humans, including understanding not just the structure of language but the structure of the world.
claims.citation: For models trained on text to be useful in general AI and scientific applications, they must understand not just the structure of language, but the structure of the world; moreover, their language, reasoning, and world knowledge capabilities must align with those in humans.

claims.entity_name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)
claims.citation: "There has been remarkable progress in AI over the past decade, but I believe the next decade will bring even greater advances in our understanding of human intelligence — advances that will reshape what we call AI."

claims.entity_name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)
claims.citation: Their AI-facing goal is to show that end-to-end explainable AI systems built using probabilistic programming can match and exceed the speed, robustness, and flexibility of human intelligence, using 100 to 1,000 times less computation than deep learning.

claims.entity_name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)

claims.entity_name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)
claims.citation: The Quest's launch materials emphasize 'thoughtful' development of AI and the importance of human values in AI systems, as expressed by advisors at the 2018 kickoff event. The initiative focuses on research and tools to ensure AI positively influences society, suggesting a balanced, responsible-development stance rather than either accelerationist or restrictive positions.

MIT Lincoln Laboratory's regulatory stance on AI is not explicitly stated in the available sources. As a DoD-affiliated FFRDC, it operates within U.S. government frameworks and its funding of AI trend research at FutureTech suggests interest in informed, evidence-based AI governance, but no direct policy positions are attributable from these sources.

claims.entity_name: MIT Task Force on the Work of the Future
claims.definition_used: Machine intelligence is the effort to recreate or even surpass human intelligence and decision-making with algorithms; the Task Force proposes focusing instead on 'machine usefulness' — technologies intended to augment human capabilities and generate new productive tasks rather than primarily eliminating human work.
claims.citation: Machine intelligence is the effort to recreate, or even surpass, human intelligence and decision making with algorithms. We propose the focus should instead be on machine usefulness: technologies intended to augment human capabilities and generate new, productive tasks rather than primarily eliminating human work.

claims.entity_name: MIT Task Force on the Work of the Future
claims.citation: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

claims.entity_name: MIT Task Force on the Work of the Future
claims.stance_label: Serious risk to workers and inequality
claims.citation: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) will surely impact inequality, but the nature of that effect depends on exactly how this technology is developed and applied. Nothing about the path of this (or any) technology is inevitable.

claims.entity_name: MIT Task Force on the Work of the Future
claims.stance_label: Moderate regulation with targeted pro-worker policies
claims.citation: To redirect AI development onto the human-complementary path requires changes in the direction of technological innovation, as well as in corporate norms and behavior. This needs to be backed up by the right priorities at the federal level and a broader public understanding of the stakes and the available choices.

claims.definition_used: AGI would produce human-level or even superhuman-level intelligence across a wide variety of cognitive tasks.
claims.citation: AGI, which would produce human-level—or even superhuman-level—intelligence across a wide variety of cognitive tasks, is plausible; it is reasonable to assume that it could be realized.

claims.definition_used: AGI is a significantly more capable AI model possessing human-level or superhuman-level intelligence across a wide variety of cognitive tasks, representing a general-purpose technology with its own agency.
claims.citation: Unlike 'narrow AI' which is built to solve a specific problem, generative AI is a general-purpose technology that has a range of potential uses. It's a form of pure cognition—and with its own agency.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: Critical infrastructure and misuse risks are serious
claims.citation: Strengthening critical infrastructure plans and promoting continuous regulatory analysis can help secure our critical infrastructure against exploitation by humans, AI-augmented humans, or malicious AI agents.

Artificial intelligence can bring precision and speed to every sector—defense, healthcare, transportation, education, and more. At the same time, AI poses potential risks to people and property, raising social, ethical, geopolitical, even existential questions.

claims.stance_label: Public concern and high-consequence use cases require assurance
claims.citation: The MITRE-Harris Poll survey on AI trends finds that just 48% of the American public believes AI is safe and secure, whereas 78% voice concern about its potential for malicious use. Assuring AI-enabled systems to address these concerns is nontrivial and will require collaboration across government, industry, and academia.

It's imperative for the U.S. government to foster accelerated AI innovation and strategic deployment—rather than stifle it—by optimizing its regulatory framework to ensure these technological capabilities align with American interests and values.

claims.stance_label: Sensible guardrails to shape AI development
claims.citation: With AI technologies growing at a staggering rate, the time is now to put best practices in place to promote AI for public good. We're drawing on six decades of experience advancing cyber and AI in the defense and civil domains and forging strategic collaborations with government, industry, and academia to inform sensible AI regulation in the near- and long-term.

By establishing a comprehensive and effective regulatory framework for AI security and safety, the incoming administration can ensure a balanced approach to technological progression, ethical considerations, and public trust.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)
Submitted as "Manageable" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

No explicit regulatory policy statements found from the organization. Co-founder Reid Hoffman has historically favored AI acceleration and innovation over heavy regulation, and the company's framing emphasizes speed, disruption, and 'abandoning things quickly that aren't working,' suggesting a light-touch preference. The company's close partnership with Microsoft and positioning as a disruptive startup further implies an innovation-first orientation.
Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

I do think that AI currently poses the most existential risk, I am more concerned than most EAs on biological risks, particularly engineered pandemics as well as bioweapons as well as very bad nuclear scenarios.

This means I am far more interested in funding direct work to detect deception in AI systems than I am in someone who wants to skill up to do AI safety research or build a meta-org to teach independent alignment researchers about epistemic or promote AI governance.
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

Artificial intelligence, as it advances inexorably, will dominate our future. It's an exciting prospect, but one that also asks the question: How do we maximize AI's potential and at the same time manage its significant risks?

claims.stance_label: Serious systemic risks at AI-world boundaries
claims.citation: As they start agenting and causing change in the world, we have to care a lot about those boundaries. [...] Unfortunately, the pattern of human history is we usually wait for something really bad to happen and then go put in the cleanup regulations after the fact, and generally overdo it.

We absolutely need to do the same thing with A.I.. When these A.I. systems intersect with the real world, the stock market, the bank payments, any system you can mention. We need to have safety and standards applying at that boundary, just as we did with Algos.

I think the regulation has to happen at these boundaries, at these intersections, at these control points first and then see where we go. And I would like to see some of these regulations in place sooner rather than later.

claims.stance_label: Standards at AI-world junctions to balance innovation and safety
claims.citation: He asserts that it will be crucial to find a balance between fostering innovation and managing risks by establishing clear and dynamic standards to govern the junctions where AI interacts with the real world.

Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from associations)

However, the company's investor base includes figures associated with accelerationist and light-touch regulatory views (e.g., Leopold Aschenbrenner's Situational Awareness LP, Andrej Karpathy), which may suggest alignment with minimal regulatory friction for AI hardware development.

claims.stance_label: AGI as not the goal; applied reasoning is
claims.definition_used: AGI is implicitly framed as a less meaningful target compared to applied, collaborative reasoning; McIlwain explicitly states the prize is not AGI but rather applied, collaborative reasoning that measurably improves outcomes.
claims.citation: The prize isn't AGI; it's applied, collaborative reasoning that measurably improves outcomes for customers, teams, and society.

claims.stance_label: AGI as not the goal; applied reasoning is
claims.definition_used: AGI is treated as a lesser objective compared to practical applied collaborative reasoning that improves enterprise outcomes.
claims.citation: The prize isn't AGI; it's applied, collaborative reasoning that measurably improves outcomes for customers, teams, and society.

Revolutions create opportunity and risk. Even with the capabilities of AI-powered reasoning machines, human nature won't change, so values, governance, explainability, and accountability matter. Pair bold deployment with strong guardrails, interpretability, auditability, and continuous learning.

claims.stance_label: Governance with guardrails, not heavy restriction
claims.citation: The importance of model and application governance, regulation, safety and transparency will expand. Creative and potentially dangerous uses of generative AI will influence the US presidential election year in unpredictable ways. Despite the expected challenges, we remain cautiously optimistic that 2024 will see many intelligent applications successfully and profitably deployed across use cases, functional areas and domains!
Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Inferred from actions)

claims.stance_label: X-risk probability estimates unreliable for policy
claims.citation: Our central claim is that AI x-risk forecasts are far too unreliable to be useful for policy, and in fact highly misleading... It would be incredibly unwise to make world-changing policy decisions based on so little evidence.

Once we looked past the alarmism, we found very little evidence that openly released advanced AI, specifically large language models, could help bad actors more than closed ones (or even the non-AI tools available to them)... While we should be cautious about the societal impact of large AI models and continue to regularly re-evaluate the risks, panic around their open release is unwarranted.
How should technology be regulated? What kind of transparency requirements should exist?... we engage directly with policy-makers to help improve society based on this understanding.

claims.stance_label: Targeted regulation of specific harms over broad restrictions
claims.citation: We think regulating the (mis)use of these models, such as cracking down on platforms used to distribute NCII, is much more justified and enforceable than regulating the development and release of the models themselves... we don't think policymakers should be rushing to put AI back in the bottle.

No explicit public statements on AI regulation were found in the sources. His work in AI safety awareness and alignment research suggests concern about AI risks, but no specific regulatory positions are documented.

claims.stance_label: AGI as flexible, autonomous, cross-context AI similar to humans
claims.definition_used: AGI refers to AI systems that do not yet exist, which process information, learn and act flexibly, autonomously and across contexts – similar to humans, and is currently defined in very different ways: from functional intelligence to conscious experience.
claims.citation: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) refers to AI systems that do not yet exist, which process information, learn and act flexibly, autonomously and across contexts – similar to humans. is currently defined in very different ways: from functional intelligence to conscious experience.

claims.stance_label: AGI capabilities are weakly emergent and reductively explainable, not agentically autonomous
claims.definition_used: AGI-like qualities attributed to AI (intelligence, creativity, agency) are weakly emergent phenomena fully explainable by underlying neural network processes; AI systems do not possess genuine autonomous agency or top-down causal powers.
claims.citation: The reductionist account of ANNs' capabilities does not deny or belittle the challenges of mitigating technological risks and allocating legal consequences for the outcomes of ANN-based systems. From this perspective, attributing qualities such as human-like agency and personality to ANN-based systems appears as bizarre as personifying other emergent phenomena, such as the movement of water molecules, protein folding, transition from gas to liquid, or crystal growth.

claims.stance_label: Serious risks requiring caution, especially from agentic/self-replicating AI

The philanthropist and longtime advocate for women and girls said she is "very nervous" about how the current AI arms race, and the rush to implement this buzzy technology into as many products as possible, will ultimately play out for women. "I'm very nervous because we don't have enough women, again, who are computer scientists, and who have expertise in artificial intelligence, and without that, we will bake bias into the system."

"The system needs to take all people's points of view, and see society, and quite frankly, see the world writ large as it is," she said. This is why having women in the room and in decision-making positions when it comes to the deployment of AI "is just so vitally important," she added.

claims.stance_label: Serious risks warrant keeping AI out of classrooms
claims.citation: Given the risks, until AI proves its worth, it's best kept out.

Yet even as phone bans spread, elected officials and Silicon Valley executives are trying to open classrooms to a technology that could set students back even further than mobile phones have: artificial intelligence.

Yet even as phone bans spread, elected officials and Silicon Valley executives are trying to open classrooms to a technology that could set students back even further than mobile phones have: artificial intelligence.

AI businesses and even policymakers are rushing to push artificial intelligence into classrooms, promising a learning revolution. But the only prudent approach is to proceed with caution. Given the risks, until AI proves its worth, it's best kept out.

Micron works with government leaders around the world to promote policies that enhance our ability to innovate, promote competitiveness and ensure that our voice is heard on the issues most important to our business interests and values.

It has the potential to solve big problems, make life easier and create new opportunities. But without the right safeguards it could also pose serious risks. If left unregulated, AI could widen inequalities, disrupt communities, put our kids at risk and accelerate environmental harm. In extreme cases, AI can be weaponised in ways that threaten global stability.

Dr Andrew Forrest co-founder of the Minderoo Foundation along with Nicola Forrest, states that the work the organisation does is to 'respond to existential threats and urgent challenges in an agile and targeted way.'

This submission argues that the framework should articulate the proposed principles and make these mandatory through statutory enactment. The UK should adopt a safety-by-design to AI innovation and ensure that regulation is informed not just by industry, but also by civil society and academia in order to foster an environment for responsible AI innovation for the public good.

claims.stance_label: Regulation of clear and obvious dangers
claims.citation: "Releasing the power of AI without regulating its clear and obvious danger is absolutely irresponsible on a historical scale," said Forrest. "You can't manage what you can't measure. Our moment in history is to ensure that the immeasurable becomes managed, that the measurable becomes regulated."

claims.stance_label: Corporate governance and regulatory ecosystem support

Its stance appears to balance aggressive investment in AI acceleration with government-directed strategic oversight, suggesting a 'developmental state' model rather than either pure acceleration or restrictive regulation.

He places the probability of achieving AGI in the next three years as 'virtually zero.' 'I could say maybe we're at a 50% or 30% chance in 10 years. Maybe by 30 years, we're in the 90% chance range,' he contends.

Jonathan Frankle: I care about the world, and I care about actually making an impact on the world. And I think all this existential risk bullshit is well, bullshit. And I think it's a great way for a lot of people to make themselves feel like they're doing something good and just keep doing what they were doing before.

I think that when it comes to policy, our job as technical folks is not to go and lead the way. Our job is to talk to the people who actually touch society, who actually see these problems happening and share our expertise, not to go and claim that we have all the answers.

Today, AI is increasingly powerful and pervasive in our society, and its promise and perils are becoming even more apparent. While there are a growing number of individuals and organizations working on making AI more trustworthy, the scope of the challenge is also continuing to grow.

claims.stance_label: Serious societal harms requiring active mitigation
claims.citation: The future is being shaped not only by Silicon Valley but by people who refuse to accept that technology has to be extractive and harmful. Mozilla Foundation funds the innovators who aren't just wrestling with tech's current issues—they're building the alternatives a better future demands.

claims.stance_label: Serious risks of concentration and harm
claims.citation: AI is one of the most transformative technologies of our time. But without strong policies, it risks becoming yet another tool for big tech consolidation and unchecked corporate power.

Clarify a federal position on open source AI export controls that maintains an open door for innovation. By affirming a federal position on open source AI export controls to reflect those of NTIA, and emphasizing the benefits of open models, the administration can spur further advancement in the field.

Accountability: Effective governance can mitigate AI's risks while fostering responsible development. Competition: Preventing monopolistic control keeps AI innovation dynamic and diverse. Openness: Encouraging open-source AI development ensures transparency, security, and accessibility.

claims.stance_label: Serious but manageable risks requiring trustworthy AI investment
claims.citation: "If we don't have that core AI stack safe and secure, then all the things we want to do for AI for good on top of it aren't going to work," said Jurgens.

claims.stance_label: Serious risks from concentrated, closed AI development
claims.citation: Today, AI is increasingly powerful and pervasive in our society, and its promise and perils are becoming even more apparent. While there are a growing number of individuals and organizations working on making AI more trustworthy, the scope of the challenge is also continuing to grow. And while we've made progress on advancing guardrails for AI systems, the AI ecosystem has also become increasingly closed and concentrated.

claims.stance_label: Targeted AI policy with openness and competition priorities
claims.citation: Promoting open source policies and approaches in AI has the potential not just to create technology that benefits individuals, but also to make AI systems safer and more transparent. Open approaches and public investment can spur increased research and development, create products that are more accessible and less vulnerable to cyberattacks, and help to catalyze investment, job creation, and a more competitive ecosystem.

"At Mozilla, we believe that comprehensive privacy legislation is foundational to any sound AI framework," Tiwari said. "Without such legislation, we risk a 'race to the bottom' where companies compete by exploiting personal data rather than safeguarding it. Maintaining U.S. leadership in AI requires America to lead on privacy and user rights."

claims.stance_label: Targeted EU AI policy with open source and accountability focus
claims.citation: Promoting Openness & Accountability in AI: Update Europe's Open Source Strategy in order to leverage the value and benefits open approaches can bring in the AI space and to create the conditions that can fuel and foster Europe's economic growth. Involve civil society, researchers, academia, and smaller AI developers in the AI Act implementation to prevent big AI companies from dominating the process.

claims.definition_used: AGI is AI capable of performing a range of tasks just like a human, surpassing the smartest individual human and eventually the collective intelligence of all humans.
claims.citation: "I think we'll have AI that is smarter than any one human probably by next year… or 2026," Musk stated. But the curve doesn't flatten there. He predicts that by 2030, "the AI will exceed the combined intelligence of all humans".

claims.definition_used: AGI is AI capable of performing a range of intellectual tasks that a human can do, with the goal of understanding the true nature of the universe.
claims.citation: "The overarching goal of xAI is to build a good AGI [artificial general intelligence] with the overarching purpose of just trying to understand the universe," Musk said.
Elon Musk also told its staff that xAI can achieve AGI as early as 2026, citing rapid progress with its Grok models.

"The overarching goal of xAI is to build a good AGI [artificial general intelligence] with the overarching purpose of just trying to understand the universe," Musk said. While Musk didn't specifically detail how xAI will be able to compete effectively against its rivals, he did outline a specific timeline in which he expects AGI to actually be a viable reality: roughly by 2029.
Elon Musk, echoing AI expert Geoffrey Hinton, estimates a 10-20% chance that artificial intelligence could cause human extinction, emphasizing both the potential benefits and existential risks of AI.

claims.stance_label: Existential risk acknowledged while building AI
claims.citation: Elon Musk is raising billions of dollars for his own supercharged AI, even though he says there's still a pretty good chance the technology backfires.
"Powerful AI systems should be developed only once we are confident that their effects will be positive and their risks will be manageable," said the letter issued by the Future of Life Institute.

"We could have a potentially catastrophic outcome," Musk said. "The most likely outcome [for AI] is positive, but we need to minimize the possibility that something could go wrong with digital superintelligence. I'm in favor of AI regulation because...advanced AI is [a] risk to the public."

claims.definition_used: AGI is AI that could theoretically rival human capabilities; NASA previously distinguished it from artificial narrow intelligence and artificial super intelligence, but has since equated it with generative AI, stating 'the terms AGI and ASI could now be viewed as generative AI (genAI) today.'
claims.citation: "NASA is looking holistically at Artificial Intelligence and not just the subparts," Dooren said. "The terms from this past framework have evolved. For example, the terms AGI and ASI could now be viewed as generative AI (genAI) today."

claims.definition_used: Human-level (or beyond) AI, with AGI as a category distinct from current narrow AI systems, and ASI as AI that surpasses human capabilities.
claims.citation: The framework focuses on concrete, practical considerations for the next five to ten years, while also beginning to lay the foundation for longer-term disruptive change as human-level (or beyond) AI is developed.

claims.stance_label: AGI is distant; terminology has evolved
claims.citation: Building artificial general intelligence — a powerful form of AI that could theoretically rival humans — is still a distant goal, but remains a key objective of companies like OpenAI and Meta.

Alongside these opportunities, all forms of AI including traditional, generative, and agentic introduce risks related to provenance, hallucination, security, and misuse. To manage these risks, NASA applies safeguards, governance processes, and validation requirements that align with federal policy, uphold mission safety, and reinforce public trust.

NASA is committed to advancing leadership in artificial intelligence and intends to maximize AI value to the Agency while managing AI risk. NASA is taking steps to expand the use of artificial intelligence and machine learning to amplify productivity and increase capabilities.
While use of AI continues to develop, NASA understands that usage must come with governance and protections for the benefit of all.
Governance provides the structured foundation that, rather than limiting innovation, accelerates its adoption and scale by addressing critical risk concerns.

claims.definition_used: AI is defined as a machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments.
claims.citation: This Strategic Plan defines AI as outlined in section 5002(3) of the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act (15 U.S.C. 9401(3)): a machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments.

claims.definition_used: AI refers to the capability of a computer system to mimic human cognitive functions such as learning and problem solving.
claims.citation: Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to the capability of a computer system to mimic human cognitive functions such as learning and problem solving. Machine Learning (ML) refers to application of AI where mathematical models of data are used to help computers learn with or without direct instruction.

claims.definition_used: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a broad concept that encompasses the idea of machines being able to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making, and translation between languages.
claims.citation: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a broad concept that encompasses the idea of machines being able to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making, and translation between languages. Machine Learning (ML) is an application (or subset) of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that provides machines (like computers) the ability to automatically learn and improve from experience, without being explicitly programmed.

claims.stance_label: AI risks addressed through responsible use policies
claims.citation: While AI may be a helpful tool in preparing applications, the rapid submission of large numbers of research applications from a single PI may undermine the fairness and originality of the research application process and unfairly strain NIH's application review processes.

claims.stance_label: Risks manageable through governance and ethical frameworks
claims.citation: NIH follows the HHS Common Rule and has its own policies to ensure the safety of human research subjects, maintain data security and quality, and provide additional protections for vulnerable [populations].

claims.stance_label: Risk-based governance framework with ethical guardrails
claims.citation: NIH's role in the HHS AI Strategic Plan will focus on conducting and funding research to advance AI in biomedical, behavioral, and health research, developing and evaluating necessary standards, supporting the development of best practices for the training of AI models, developing and training AI workforce, and promoting the responsible use of AI.
Applications that are either substantially developed by AI or containing sections substantially developed by AI are not considered the original ideas of applicants and will not be considered by NIH.

NOT-OD-23-149: Informs the extramural community that the NIH prohibits NIH scientific peer reviewers from using natural language processors, large language models, or other generative AI technologies for analyzing and formulating peer review critiques for grant applications and R&D contract proposals.

claims.citation: The term 'artificial intelligence' means a machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions influencing real or virtual environments. Artificial intelligence systems use machine and human-based inputs to: (A) Perceive real and virtual environments. (B) Abstract such perceptions into models through analysis in an automated manner. (C) Use model inference to formulate options for information or action.

claims.stance_label: AI risks real but manageable through research and governance
claims.citation: As with any powerful and complex tool, AI comes with risks; responsibility for managing such risks is shared across all phases of the AI life cycle, including R&D. Supporting research on AI's societal implications, developing testing and evaluation approaches, improving auditing capabilities, and developing best practices for responsible AI R&D can help improve understanding and yield tools to manage AI risks.

claims.stance_label: Risks confined through technical mitigations and human governance
claims.citation: We are focused on maximizing AI's benefits and distributing them broadly across society, confining the risks through technical mitigations and human governance and empowering a diversity of individuals and organizations to participate in AI innovation.

claims.stance_label: Safe, secure, and trustworthy AI as guiding framework
claims.citation: The NAIRR pilot will initially support AI research to advance safe, secure and trustworthy AI, as well as the application of AI to challenges in healthcare and environmental and infrastructure sustainability.

claims.stance_label: Targeted governance with responsible AI standards
claims.citation: The NAIRR must be proactive in addressing privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties issues by integrating appropriate technical controls, policies... The NAIRR should implement system safeguards in accordance with established guidelines.
